article_title: Endoscope-assisted retrosigmoid approach in hemifacial spasm: our experience☆
label: Figure 1
caption: Minimally invasive retrosigmoid approach (right side). The image shows the anatomic landmarks for the surgical access: (1) Frankfurt plane between the external canthus and tragus superior edge; (2) digastrics’ muscle plane; (3) craniotomy site projection; (4) surgical incision.

label: Figure 2
caption: Endoscopic view of the neurovascular conflict before the decompression (A) and after Teflon® sponge interposition between the posterior–inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) and the facial nerve (B).

label: Figure 3
caption: Frequency of the different vessels involved in the neurovascular conflict with the facial nerve in our study group. PICA, posterior–inferior cerebellar artery; AICA, anterior–inferior cerebellar artery.

label: Figure 4
caption: Frequency of the different neurovascular conflict sites in our series. REZ, root entry zone; IAC, internal auditory canal; N/A, non-arterial conflict (vein).

article_title: Endoscope-assisted retrosigmoid approach in hemifacial spasm: our experience☆
label: Figure 6
caption: T2 weighted MRI showing the boundaries of the root entry zone (REZ) in the cerebellopontine angle and the internal auditory canal (IAC). Notice the conflict between the vessel (white arrow) and the facial nerve (black arrow) at the REZ.

article_title: Endoscope-assisted retrosigmoid approach in hemifacial spasm: our experience☆
label: Figure 7
caption: Endoscopic (A, B) and microscopic (C, D) image of a neurovascular conflict before the decompression (A, C) and after Teflon® sheet interposition between the vascular loop and the facial nerve (B, D). It is important to notice the difference in details resolution and anatomical structures definition between endoscopic full-HD technology and microscopic vision. VII, facial nerve; VIII, Statoacoustic nerve; XII, hypoglossal nerve; T, Teflon® sponge; v, vein.

article_title: Long-term surgical results in microvascular decompression for hemifacial spasm: efficacy, morbidity and quality of life
label: Fig. 1.
caption: Pre- and post-operative HFS-8 score. The median is indicated by a thick black line inside the box. The top and bottom of the box are the upper and lower quartile [25th-75th percentiles] and indicate the inter-quartile range (IQR). The whiskers indicate the lowest/highest datum still within 1.5 IQR of the lower/upper quartile. Outliers are marked with a cross. HSF-8 score was significantly improved after surgery (*** p < 0.001).

article_title: Retrospective clinical analysis of 320 cases of microvascular decompression for hemifacial spasm
label: Figure 1
caption: (A–F) The surgical processes are partially shown. Green arrow: anterior inferior cerebellar artery. Blue arrow: auditory nerve. Orange arrow: facial nerve. Red arrow: lower cranial nerves (vagus nerve, accessory nerve, and hypoglossal nerve). Purple arrow: labyrinthine artery.

article_title: Comparison of Surgical Outcomes in Microscopic and Fully Endoscopic Microvascular Decompression for Hemifacial Spasm
label: Fig. 1
caption: Axial MRI (constructive interference in steady state) before endoscopic MVD.In A and B, the left image was from near the jugular foramen level, the right image from the IAM level, and the middle image from in between the jugular foramen and the IAM, depicting the area near the REZ of the facial nerve.A: Three consecutive MRIs of a representative patient (44-year-old man) in whom endoscopic MVD was deemed difficult. MRI displayed proximity of the clivus and petrosal bone to the pons, including the REZ. However, we chose endoscopic MVD because the shape of the posterior wall of the petrous bone was smooth, with few obstructions in the entry route of the endoscopic instruments. On the axial image at the REZ of CN VII, the cerebellopontine cistern space was relatively large (arrowhead), and endoscopic instruments could be inserted after the cerebrospinal fluid was drained.B: Three consecutive MRIs of a representative patient (57-year-old woman) in whom endoscopic MVD was deemed suitable. MRI confirmed that there was sufficient space in the cerebellopontine cistern for endoscopic manipulation on the basis of the small size of the flocculus and brain stem, and cerebellar atrophy, and on the basis of the age of the patient.Lt: left; SS: sigmoid sinus; CN VII: cranial nerve seven (facial nerve); IAM: internal auditory meatus; MRI: magnetic resonance imaging; MVD: microvascular decompression; REZ: root exit zone
